preparation_method: tempering (tadka) with mustard seeds and curry leaves

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of buttermilk (chaas) is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing around 90 kcal, 5.5 g protein, 3 g fat, and 10 g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Buttermilk (chaas) values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods'. Saturated fat and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, FDC ID 01088, Buttermilk, fluid, cultured, lowfat) as ICMR-NIN does not report detailed fatty acid fractions. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in either source for this specific preparation and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Yogurt-based beverages (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Indian chaas (spiced buttermilk) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to plain low-fat yogurt drinks and cultured dairy beverages (GI range 11–36 per University of Sydney GI Database). The high dilution with water, lactic acid content, and absence of added sugar strongly support a low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 4.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.5. Confidence: 0.70 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sodium from salt seasoning (120 mg per 100 g; ~300 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request or prepare chaas with reduced or no added salt, as the standard preparation contributes approximately 300 mg sodium per serving, which is 13% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate content (4 g per 100 g), with no added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chaas is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI, minimal carbohydrate load (GL ~1.5 per 250 ml serving), and probiotic content from yogurt, which may support glycemic regulation; however, commercially prepared versions should be checked for added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8 g per 100 g), low cholesterol (11 mg per 100 g), and probiotic content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chaas is a heart-friendly beverage with low saturated fat and cholesterol; its probiotic content may support favorable lipid profiles, but individuals on sodium-restricted cardiac diets should limit or eliminate added salt in preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotic content and anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cumin)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The probiotic bacteria from yogurt and anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and cumin may support gut health and reduce systemic inflammation; individuals should monitor personal dairy tolerance.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Fermentation reduces lactose content compared to fresh milk
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Chaas is often better tolerated than fresh milk due to partial lactose breakdown during yogurt fermentation; individuals with severe lactose intolerance should exercise caution and start with small quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buttermilk (Chaas) is a traditional Indian yogurt-based beverage consumed across the Indian subcontinent, particularly in Gujarat, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and other regions. It is prepared by churning yogurt with cold water to a thin, watery consistency — distinct from the thicker, sweeter lassi — and is characteristically savory. The drink is seasoned with salt, roasted cumin powder, fresh ginger, and green chilies. A common regional variation involves a 'tadka' (tempering) of mustard seeds and curry leaves fried briefly in a small amount of oil, which is then stirred into the drink, adding a warm, aromatic depth. The flavor profile is tangy, lightly spiced, and refreshing. Nutritionally, chaas is low in calories (36 kcal per 100 g), provides moderate protein (2.2 g), low fat (1.2 g), and minimal carbohydrates (4 g) with no added sugar. It is a good source of calcium (72 mg per 100 g) and riboflavin, and contains live probiotic cultures from the yogurt base that support digestive health. Its very low glycemic index (~15) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (120 mg per 100 g) from added salt warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, who should request reduced-salt preparations. Chaas is a staple accompaniment to Indian meals, valued for aiding digestion, cooling the body in hot climates, and rehydrating after physical activity. It is naturally gluten-free, vegetarian, and halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a glass of chaas?
Is Indian buttermilk the same as Western buttermilk?
What is the difference between lassi and chaas?
What is the GI of buttermilk?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', the primary approved source for Indian foods. Fatty acid fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 01088). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cultured dairy beverages, with documented estimation basis. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a diluted yogurt-based beverage.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Indian spiced buttermilk (chaas) is not reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 01088) for this specific preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for chaas as prepared (with variable water dilution ratio) is not standardized in ICMR-NIN or USDA sources. trans_fat_g value of 0.04 g per 100 g is a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in dairy; this is not an industrial trans fat. The input record listed saturated_fat and trans_fat sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden source); these values have been retained only because they are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cultured low-fat buttermilk and are documented as cross-referenced. The dietary_tag 'low_sodium' in the input is inconsistent with 120 mg sodium per 100 g (300 mg per serving); this tag has been removed. The dietary_tag 'keto' has been removed as chaas contains 4 g carbohydrate per 100 g (10 g per serving), which is not consistent with strict ketogenic dietary thresholds. Duplicate portions in the input (three identical '1 serving (250ml)' entries) have been deduplicated to a single default serving of 250 g. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chaas exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024.
